title: Welding Discontinuities: Types, Origin, and Orientation
narrationScript: Last lesson we treated the weld as a small casting and learned how each process pours it. Now we meet the defects themselves. The theme of this lesson is simple and powerful. Every weld discontinuity is born from a specific physical event, and that event fixes where it lives and which way it faces. If you know the birth story, you know the address and the orientation, and orientation is everything to an angle beam. Start with the cracks, because cracks are the flaws that break things. Hot cracks form while the weld is still solidifying. As the puddle freezes, grains grow inward from each sidewall, and the last liquid, loaded with low melting impurities like sulfur, gets squeezed into the centerline. Shrinkage pulls the bead apart along that weak center plane. So solidification cracks run down the middle of the bead, usually vertical and longitudinal, and they favor deep narrow beads like submerged arc passes. Stop the arc carelessly and the same mechanism makes a small star shaped crater crack at the end of the bead. Cold cracks are a different animal. They need three ingredients. Hydrogen from moisture in flux or rust or grease. A hardened brittle microstructure, usually in the heat-affected zone of a hardenable steel. And tensile stress from weld shrinkage. The crack pops hours or even days after welding, which is why codes often require the final ultrasonic examination of high strength steel to wait twenty-four or forty-eight hours after the weld cools. Cold cracks live in the heat-affected zone, as underbead cracks roughly parallel to the fusion line, or break the surface at the weld toe where stress concentrates. One more relative, lamellar tearing, happens in the base metal itself. When a tee or corner joint pulls through the thickness of a plate that contains flattened inclusions, the plate delaminates in a step like pattern parallel to its surface, just below the heat-affected zone. Next, the fusion family. Lack of fusion means a bead lay against the sidewall or a previous pass without melting into it. It comes from a cold puddle, fast travel, poor angle, or metal rolling ahead of the arc, and it is the signature flaw of short circuit wire feed welding. It is planar, smooth, and it lies parallel to the surface it failed to fuse, which usually means parallel to the bevel face. Incomplete penetration means the root never fused at all. Too big a root face, too small a root opening, too little heat, or a misaligned pass leaves an unwelded slot at the root of a single vee weld, on the centerline, essentially vertical. In a double vee welded from both sides, the same failure hides at mid thickness where the two sides were supposed to meet. Then the volumetric crowd. Slag inclusions are trapped flux residue, found along sidewalls and between passes where cleaning was poor, often elongated into lines that follow the welding direction, with irregular surfaces. Porosity is trapped gas from lost shielding, moisture, or surface contamination. It comes as isolated spheres, clusters, aligned strings along a pass, or elongated wormholes called piping. Because pores are smooth and round, they scatter sound rather than mirror it, a point that matters next lesson. Tungsten inclusions are dense little fragments left when a TIG electrode touches the puddle. Finally the surface and geometry family. Undercut is a groove melted into the base metal along the toe. Underfill is a cap or root left below flush. Overlap is metal rolled over the surface without fusion. Excessive penetration hangs icicles below the root, and misalignment, called high low, steps one member above the other. These matter twice. They are rejectable in their own right under workmanship rules, and they generate honest geometric echoes that you must not confuse with buried defects. Pull the thread tight and the pattern is clear. Discontinuities inherit their geometry from whatever made them. Fusion defects follow the fusion faces. Solidification defects follow the centerline. Hydrogen defects follow the heat affected zone. Plate defects follow the plate. Round gas follows nothing. That map of locations and orientations is exactly the scan plan you built last lesson, and next lesson we will see how each of these characters answers the beam.
imagePrompt: An inspector at a metallurgical bench examines a polished and etched cross-section of a multi-pass V-groove weld under a magnifying lamp, with bend-test coupons and a hardness tester in the background.

sections.heading: Why Origin Predicts Position
sections.body:
Every welding discontinuity is created by a specific physical event — liquid metal tearing as it freezes, hydrogen embrittling a hardened zone, flux that never floated out, gas that never escaped, a sidewall that never melted. That birth mechanism fixes **where the flaw sits and which way its plane faces**, and for an angle-beam method that lives on specular reflection, orientation is everything.

Keep the standard wording straight as you work: a **discontinuity** is any interruption in the part's normal structure; an **indication** is what your instrument shows; a discontinuity only becomes a **defect** when it exceeds the acceptance criteria of the governing code. Your job in this lesson is the middle step — knowing, before the probe ever touches steel, what population of discontinuities this weld could realistically contain, so the scan plan interrogates each candidate near-normal to its expected plane.

sections.caption: Atlas of a multi-pass single-V butt weld showing the typical home of each major discontinuity type.

sections.heading: Cracks: Hot, Cold, and Delayed
sections.body:
**Solidification (hot) cracks** form while the bead freezes. Grains grow inward from each sidewall and sweep low-melting segregates (sulfur, phosphorus) toward the center; shrinkage strain then tears the weak last-to-freeze plane. Result: a **centerline crack**, longitudinal and essentially vertical in the bead — favored by deep, narrow beads (SAW) and by high travel speeds. The same mechanism at an abrupt arc stop leaves a small star-shaped **crater crack**.

**Hydrogen (cold) cracks** need three ingredients together: hydrogen (damp electrodes or flux, rust, oil), a **hardened microstructure** — usually the HAZ of a hardenable steel — and tensile residual stress. They are **delayed**, appearing hours to days after cooling, which is why fabrication codes commonly hold the final UT of high-strength steel welds for **24–48 hours**. Typical forms: **underbead cracks** in the HAZ roughly parallel to the fusion line, and **toe cracks** starting at the cap toe's stress concentration, often nearly vertical.

**Lamellar tearing** is a base-metal failure: through-thickness shrinkage at T- and corner joints delaminates plate along flattened inclusion stringers, producing a **step-like tear parallel to the plate surface** just outside the HAZ. **Transverse cracks** — across the weld axis — occur in highly restrained, high-strength welds and are notorious for being missed by standard scans, which is why coverage plans add transverse passes.

sections.heading: Fusion-Type Discontinuities: LOF and IP
sections.body:
**Lack of fusion (LOF)** is weld metal lying against a surface it never melted: the groove sidewall, a previous pass, or the backing. Causes are a cold puddle (low amperage, short-circuit GMAW), travel too fast, bad electrode angle, or molten metal rolling ahead of the arc. LOF is **planar and relatively smooth**, and it lies **parallel to the surface it failed to fuse** — on the bevel that usually means a plane about 30° off vertical, mid-wall, on one side of the weld. It is a strong mirror to a beam that hits it square and nearly invisible to one that doesn't.

**Incomplete penetration (IP)** means the root was never fused. An oversized root face, an undersized root opening, low heat, or a misaligned root pass leaves an unwelded slot **on the weld centerline at the root** of a single-V joint — planar, **essentially vertical**, often surface-connected on the far side, and frequently a fierce reflector because it forms a corner with the far surface. In **double-V or double-bevel joints welded from both sides**, the equivalent failure sits at **mid-thickness** where the two sides were supposed to meet (incomplete joint penetration), unless back-gouging removed it. Misalignment (hi-lo) raises the odds of root LOF/IP on the low side.

sections.heading: Slag, Porosity, and the Surface Family
sections.body:
**Slag inclusions** are flux residue that never floated out — a risk only for slag-forming processes (SMAW, FCAW, SAW). They lodge along **sidewalls and between passes**, especially in undercut grooves left by a previous bead, and are often **elongated into lines running with the welding direction**, with rough, irregular surfaces.

**Porosity** is trapped gas: lost shielding (wind on site GMAW), moisture, or contamination. Forms: **isolated** pores, **cluster** porosity, **aligned/linear** porosity tracking a contaminated pass (often the root), and elongated **piping or wormhole** porosity. Pores are smooth spheres — weak, scattered reflectors. **Tungsten inclusions** (GTAW) are small dense fragments from a dipped electrode.

The **surface and geometry family** matters twice — rejectable in itself and a source of honest geometric signals you must not mistake for buried flaws:

- **Undercut** — a groove melted into base metal along the toe (or root) and left unfilled
- **Underfill** — cap or root surface below flush
- **Overlap (cold lap)** — metal rolled over the surface without fusion
- **Excessive penetration / burn-through** — root metal sagging below the far surface
- **Misalignment (hi-lo)** — offset members stepping the root

Each produces a repeatable echo tied to the weld profile; lesson ut2-4-3 covers telling them from defects.

sections.caption: Where each discontinuity lives and how it is oriented.
rows: SAW deep beads, high travel speed
Parallel to fusion face (~30° off vertical)
Big root face, small root opening, hi-lo
Anywhere; root pass and restarts favored

sections.caption: Orientation map — the plane each discontinuity presents in cross-section and in plan view, with the beam direction that interrogates it best.

sections.title: Orientation drives detectability
sections.body: Planar flaws are mirrors: a beam within a few degrees of normal incidence gets a strong specular return, while 15–20° of misorientation can drop the echo by more than 12 dB. So the scan plan is really an orientation plan — **60° beams square onto 30° bevels for LOF, corner-trapping beams at the root for IP, transverse passes for transverse cracks**. If a weld could contain a flaw whose plane your beam never meets near-normal, your coverage has a hole regardless of how carefully you scanned.

checkQuestions.question: Why do fabrication codes often require the final UT of high-strength steel welds to be delayed 24–48 hours after welding?
options: To let the couplant cure on the surface
Because hydrogen (cold) cracking is delayed and may not exist yet when the weld first cools
To allow residual ultrasound from welding to dissipate
Because slag inclusions take two days to form
checkQuestions.explanation: Cold cracks need hydrogen, a hardened HAZ, and tensile stress, and they initiate hours to days after cooling. Testing immediately can pass a weld that cracks the next day.

checkQuestions.question: Sidewall lack of fusion in a single-V groove typically presents as a plane that is:
options: Horizontal, parallel to the plate surfaces
Random — LOF has no preferred orientation
Parallel to the bevel face, roughly 30° off vertical, on one side of the weld
Always on the weld centerline
checkQuestions.explanation: LOF copies the surface that failed to fuse — the bevel — so it lies along the fusion face. That is why probe angles are chosen to strike the bevel near-normal.

checkQuestions.question: In a double-V butt weld made from both sides without back-gouging, incomplete joint penetration would most likely be found:
options: At mid-thickness on the centerline, where the two sides should meet
At the cap toe
On the far-surface root, as in a single-V weld
In the base metal outside the HAZ
checkQuestions.explanation: Each side penetrates toward the middle, so the unfused region hides at mid-wall on the centerline — a vertical planar reflector with no surface connection.

checkQuestions.question: Lamellar tearing is best described as:
options: A weld-metal centerline crack caused by sulfur segregation
A step-like base-metal separation parallel to the plate surface under highly restrained T- or corner joints
Spherical gas pockets in the root pass
A vertical crack starting at the weld toe
checkQuestions.explanation: Through-thickness shrinkage strain pulls plate apart along flattened inclusion stringers, producing stepped tears parallel to the surface in the base metal — not in the weld metal.

checkQuestions.question: Which process condition is most strongly associated with cold lap / lack of fusion?
options: GTAW with a fresh tungsten electrode
SAW with excessive flux depth
SMAW with low-hydrogen electrodes straight from the oven
GMAW in short-circuit transfer with low heat input
checkQuestions.explanation: Short-circuit GMAW runs a relatively cold puddle that can lie against the sidewall or prior pass without fusing — the classic origin of LOF/cold lap.

references: CP-105-2024 UT Evaluation Course §3.4 (origin and typical orientation of discontinuities)
Discontinuity terminology per documents such as AWS A3.0 and ASNT training materials (cited generically)
Delayed-inspection requirements for high-strength steels per structural codes such as AWS D1.1 (cited generically)
